Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty scattered, which I'm not proud of. With 15 online courses to juggle and limited resources, I had to be strategic about when and where I could access materials. I usually tried to batch my study sessions on days when I could get to the library or somewhere with reliable internet. Being 28 and having other responsibilities meant I couldn't always stick to an ideal schedule, but I aimed for about 17 hours a week when I could manage it.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on deadlines and what else was going on in my life. Some weeks I'd be really consistent, studying in the evenings after work or other commitments. But then deadlines would pile up and I'd have to cram everything into a few intense sessions. The irregularity was stressful, but I've learned to work with what I've got at this point in my life.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline approaching or wanting to catch up on discussion posts that prompted me to log in. I'm an auditory learner, so I'd specifically log in to watch video lectures since that's how information really sticks for me. Sometimes I'd get notifications about new content or peer responses to my posts, and that would pull me back in. The discussion forums actually kept me coming back fairly regularly.
Q4: Yeah, after a few weeks, checking the LMS became part of my morning routine, like checking email. I'd log in during my commute or first thing when I had internet access to see what was new. The discussion posts really helped cement this habit because I genuinely wanted to see what others were saying. It became almost automatic to check in, even on days when I wasn't planning to do heavy studying.
Q5: The video lectures were absolutely essential for me since I learn best by listening. I'd often replay sections multiple times to really absorb the material. The discussion forums were also incredibly valuable because hearing different perspectives helped me process concepts in a way that just reading couldn't. I also found the peer interactions motivating, even when I was feeling low on motivation otherwise.
Q6: I'll admit I often skipped or delayed the reading materials, especially dense PDFs. With my auditory learning style, reading just doesn't stick as well, and with limited resources, I had to prioritize what worked best for me. Some of the supplementary materials felt redundant after watching the videos, so I'd skim them at best. I know my attendance wasn't great at under 40%, but I tried to make up for it with the resources that actually worked for my learning style.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could actually hear or discuss the content rather than just read it passively. If it connected directly to assignments or exams, that obviously helped justify the time investment. I also valued activities where I could interact with peers because that social element kept me engaged even when motivation was low. Basically, if I could tell it would help my understanding or my grade, I'd prioritize it.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't always afford, honestly. With 15 courses and limited time and resources, I had to be ruthless about what I engaged with. Required activities got done because they had to, but optional ones I'd only do if they genuinely interested me or if I had extra time. The mindfulness exercises they offered were technically optional, but I found those really helpful for managing my stress, so I made time for those.
Q9: Absolutely, I did a lot of studying offline that wouldn't show up anywhere. I'd download videos and audio files when I had good internet access, then study them later at home or on the go. I kept handwritten notes from lectures that I'd review constantly. I also recorded myself summarizing concepts and listened to those recordings repeatedly - that's probably my most effective study method, but it's completely invisible to any system.
Q10: There were many times I was deeply engaged with course material but the LMS wouldn't know. I'd download a lecture once, then listen to it five or six times over the following days while commuting or doing chores. I'd have long conversations with classmates outside the platform about course concepts. Some of my best learning happened while walking and mentally reviewing what I'd heard in lectures - no clicks, no logins, just thinking.
Q11: This was basically my standard approach, yeah. I'd access materials once to download them, then continue studying offline for days. Given my inconsistent internet access and limited resources, downloading everything possible in one session was essential. My video watch time might look high, but a lot of that was me downloading rather than streaming in real-time. The real learning happened later, offline, sometimes over and over again.
Q12: There were definitely periods when engaging became really hard, usually when multiple deadlines converged or when I was just feeling sad and unmotivated. Being 28 with financial constraints means life doesn't pause for coursework. Sometimes I'd go through stretches where I felt disconnected from the material and my peers, and logging in felt like a chore. The mindfulness intervention actually helped me push through some of those darker periods.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted or stressed, I'd do the bare minimum on the LMS - just enough to not fall behind completely. Competing responsibilities as an older student meant coursework sometimes had to take a backseat. Confusion with course material would sometimes make me avoid logging in because I didn't want to face what I didn't understand. Fatigue especially affected my evening study sessions, which is when I often planned to engage with content.
Q14: Oh, plenty of times. I'd have the LMS open, videos playing, but my mind would be elsewhere - worrying about money, thinking about other obligations, or just feeling that persistent sadness. I'd technically be 'watching' content but not absorbing anything. Sometimes I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them, just going through the motions. Those sessions felt hollow, like I was performing engagement rather than actually learning.
Q15: I postponed things regularly, especially written assignments and readings. Even knowing they were important, I'd tell myself I'd do them later when I felt more motivated or less sad. The assignments I completed at about 74% - that gap represents all the times I avoided things until it was too late. Sometimes the avoidance was about fear of not doing well; other times I just couldn't muster the energy. My low resilience meant setbacks hit me hard and made me want to avoid similar situations.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my external motivation since my internal motivation was pretty low. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly my LMS activity would spike dramatically. Most of my engagement was concentrated in the days right before things were due. It wasn't ideal, but deadlines gave me the structure I needed to actually get things done. Without them, I probably would have drifted even more.
Q17: Feedback had a complicated effect on me. Positive feedback would give me a temporary boost and make me want to engage more. But critical feedback, even constructive criticism, would sometimes make me withdraw for a bit because of my low resilience. I tried to use feedback to improve my subsequent work, but emotionally it could be tough to process. The timing mattered too - quick feedback kept me connected to the course.
Q18: The course structure with its regular deadlines and discussion requirements actually helped me stay somewhat on track despite my low motivation. Having 15 courses meant I needed clear structure or I'd get completely lost. The discussion post requirements were good because they forced regular engagement with peers. However, the structure didn't account for students like me who do a lot of learning offline, so my participation might have looked lower than my actual engagement.
Q19: The video-heavy content made engagement much easier for me as an auditory learner. The discussion forums were well-designed and made peer interaction feel natural. What made it harder was when materials were only available in text format or when there were technical barriers given my limited resources. The pacing sometimes felt relentless with 15 courses - more flexibility in deadlines would have helped during my harder weeks.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is trying to understand material but struggling to produce work that meets their standards. They might be researching, watching videos, reading discussions, but feeling too uncertain or anxious to actually submit anything. Or they could be dealing with personal issues that make it hard to complete work even though they're trying to stay connected. High activity without submissions might also indicate someone who's lost or confused about requirements.
Q21: That definitely reflects my experience to some degree. My attendance was under 40% and I wasn't always visibly active, but I still managed a Merit grade and decent exam scores. A lot of my learning happened offline with downloaded materials and my own audio recordings. The LMS only captures one dimension of engagement. Someone could be deeply processing course material through methods that leave no digital trace at all.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show patterns of when students access materials and how often they participate in discussions. They're good at tracking deadline-driven behavior and identifying students who might be completely disengaged. But they miss all the offline learning - the downloaded videos rewatched multiple times, the peer discussions happening outside the platform, the mental processing that happens away from screens. They also can't distinguish between mindless clicking and genuine engagement.
Q23: For me, being engaged means actually thinking about and processing the course material, not just clicking through it. It's when I'm making connections between concepts, discussing ideas with peers, and applying what I'm learning. True engagement is when the content occupies my thoughts even when I'm not actively studying - when I'm mentally reviewing while walking or connecting course concepts to real life. It's about depth of processing, not frequency of logins.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I relied on audio-based learning and self-recorded summaries. It wouldn't capture the mindfulness practices that helped me manage my stress and sadness enough to keep going. It wouldn't show the peer conversations that happened over text or in person, or the times I was deeply engaged with downloaded content offline. Most importantly, it wouldn't show the emotional effort it took to persist through periods of low motivation and sadness while managing limited resources.
